title: Boxing for Beginners With Rage
dek: When the jaw aches and the fists curl in the dark, talk therapy is not enough. Here is how to wrap your hands, strike the heavy bag, and let your biology complete the cycle of protection it started years ago.
bodyMarkdown:
You lie awake at 3 AM, the night so still it presses against your skin like a damp sheet. The air smells faintly of cold concrete and the faint trace of lavender from the sachet tucked under your pillow. You can feel it there: the slow, insistent tightening of your masseter muscles, the thick bands of tension that lock your jaw in place. It’s a clenched cage around your teeth, a silent scream held just behind your molars. Your fists are not at rest either. Curling, uncurling, knuckles whitening with the effort of holding in a storm that wants to break loose. This is the body’s language of fury, unspoken but unmistakable.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.079Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You were taught that this feeling was ugly, something to be ashamed of, something to swallow and hide. But here’s the truth: this is not a moral failure. This is biology. Your body remembers, even when your mind tries to forget. The masseter muscle, that thick, powerful muscle responsible for chewing, clamps down like a trap when rage wells up. It’s the first fortress of your untamed fire, a muscle that doesn’t just tighten - it commands. The tension stretches like a chain through your trapezius and levator scapulae, pulling your shoulders up and forward, as if preparing you to fight or flee in the dead of night.

The urge to strike is not just a metaphor. It is a physical impulse that demands release. Your fists, those gathered bundles of tendons and sinew, want to move, to connect with something solid, to express what words cannot convey. You are not broken for feeling this. The body knows what the mind can’t always say. This is not pathology; this is biology. The rage that coils in the psoas muscle deep in your abdomen, the way your diaphragm tightens as if trying to hold your breath, all these are the body’s way of signaling: something must shift.

When fury is unexpressed, it becomes a weight lodged deep in your fascia, a hidden ache beneath the skin that no one else can see but you feel in every fiber. It’s the reason your neck stiffens when you wake, the reason your hands tremble slightly in moments of quiet. Rage is not a flaw in your character; it is your body’s urgent message, a pulse that insists on being acknowledged.

Boxing is not about violence. It is about reclaiming that energy with intention and precision. It is about learning to listen to the tension in your masseter, to the coil in your fists, and to translate it into movement that frees rather than fractures. When you punch a bag, you’re not just breaking down the leather and canvas - you’re breaking down the old stories you were told about your fury. You’re inviting your body to move through the tension rather than holding it hostage.

This practice is a radical act of self-respect. You honor the biology of your rage without shame, without judgment. You allow the muscles - the masseter, the trapezius, the levator scapulae, the psoas, the diaphragm - to tell their story through motion. You learn to hear the language of your body, to recognize that the tightening at 3 AM is not your enemy but your ally, asking you to engage rather than suppress.

Here’s the thing: rage is medicine. It is a force that can carve new pathways through your nervous system. It can be a tool of transformation when wielded with care. This article is for the women who have been taught their fury was ugly, unworthy, or dangerous. It’s for you, standing in the quiet dark, jaw clenched, fists restless, wondering if there’s a way to hold your rage without it holding you. There is. And it begins with learning to move through it, not around it.

## The Biology of the Strike

It’s late afternoon; the light outside softens into that dusty gold that only spring can hold, and you’re in the gym, gloves wrapped tight around your hands. Your body hums with quiet tension, but not the kind that knots and freezes you in place. This is readiness, a different animal altogether. The latissimus dorsi on your back - those broad, fan-shaped muscles that cradle your ribs - presses into the fascia like a drawn bowstring, ready to launch. Meanwhile, deep in your abdomen, the psoas muscles stiffen and coil, their long fibers connecting your spine to your legs like a hidden cable system, stabilizing your core for the action to come. This is not just movement; it is a reclamation encoded in your very cells.

You strike. The punch moves through the masseter muscle in your jaw, which clenches instinctively, the trapezius and levator scapulae in your neck and shoulders bracing, channeling force and intention through your arm. Each part of your body knows its role, from the grounding of your feet in worn leather boxing shoes to the final snap of your fist cutting through the air. This is the biology of the strike, and it makes profound sense for the nervous system, especially for those of us whose fury was once shamed into silence.

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, teaches us that trauma is not stored in the abstract corridors of the brain alone - it is held in the body, in the muscles that tighten, the breath that shortens, the nervous system that remains trapped in a state of freeze or hypervigilance. Trauma is unfinished business in the nervous system, a defensive response that never got completed. When a threat appeared, your body prepared to fight or flee, but the action was interrupted or suppressed. The jaw clenched, the shoulders hiked, the breath held, but the punch never landed. You were taught to swallow that fury, to keep it hidden beneath a polite smile or a tight-lipped quiet. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Boxing, then, offers a radical form of somatic justice. It provides a space for the defensive response cycle to complete itself. When you throw a punch, your nervous system receives the signal that the threat has been met, that the defense is no longer stalled in the body. The latissimus dorsi contracts fully, sending a ripple of satisfaction through your back. The psoas, often misunderstood as a muscle solely of movement, here acts as a keeper of visceral memory - it tightens before the strike, then releases in the follow-through, a visceral exhale of tension that speaks to the nervous system’s recalibration. This is a language your body understands deeply.

The punch is more than physical - it is the soundless scream that was never allowed, the boundary set with fierce clarity, the reclaimed right to anger that courses through your veins like molten iron. The muscles involved - the masseter clenching and unclenching, the trapezius and levator scapulae riding the wave of impact - are not just mechanical actors. They are the storytellers of your nervous system’s journey from captivity to freedom.

In this practice, you are not just hitting bags or sparring with opponents; you are engaging in a dialogue with your own biology. The strike becomes a ritual of release and empowerment, a way to tell your nervous system “this wound is held, this fury is known, and now it moves.” The body remembers what the mind sometimes cannot say aloud. The psoas and latissimus dorsi, muscles often implicated in chronic tension and pain, participate in a healing choreography that honors your rage instead of shaming it.

You may notice the smell of sweat mingling with the faint, earthy scent of the herbal salve you applied earlier - made from St. John’s Wort and Arnica, herbs that your teacher, herbalist Robin Rose Bennett, swears by for calming inflammation and promoting tissue repair. The smell anchors you in the physicality of the moment, grounding you as you repeat the cycle - strike, breathe, release. This is the biology of the strike, a visceral reclamation that rewrites the narrative stored in your fascia and muscle fibers.

You are not violent; you are whole. You are not angry without cause; you are responding to a body that remembers, a nervous system that knows. The punch, when wielded with intention and respect, becomes a vessel for truth, a somatic map to healing. And in this room, in this moment, the biology of your strike tells a story your fury has always demanded you tell: I am here. I am alive. I will no longer be silenced.

## Wrapping the Bones

You start by sitting on the edge of a creaky wooden bench, the kind that’s been around since before you were born, its varnish worn thin where countless others have sat, lacing up their gloves or just catching breath. The basement smells like a mix of old cement and the sharp, almost bitter tang of leather - leather gloves, the heavy bag, the floor mats. There’s a faint undertone of sweat, not yet sour, more like a quiet promise of work to come. It’s early evening, just past six, and the air feels cool but not cold, the kind of dampness that clings to skin and makes the muscles beneath it want to move.

You pull out your hand wraps, those long strips of cotton that feel rough against your skin but soften with use, like an old friend. The ritual of wrapping is where the fight begins - not with the fists, but with the bones beneath. You start with the wrist, wrapping around the distal radius and ulna, feeling the subtle give and the steady pulse of the radial artery beneath your fingers. The wrapping isn’t just about protection; it’s a conversation with your body. You’re telling your wrist, “I see you. I’m holding you steady.” You cross the wrap over the back of your hand, encasing the metacarpals, paying particular attention to the spaces between the knuckles, especially the second and third, where the masseter muscle’s tension sometimes radiates - a reminder from your jaw that the body remembers every clench and every unspoken word.

Each pass of the wrap presses gently into the fascia over the knuckles, compressing but not cutting off circulation. That firm embrace around the carpals and metacarpals is a grounding, an alignment of the skeletal frame that will take the impact of your fury. You think of the trapezius and levator scapulae muscles in your neck, already tightening at the anticipation, and you breathe deeply into your diaphragm, the breath pushing down into your belly, expanding the psoas muscles nestled deep along your spine and pelvis. This breath is the first strike, a centering force before the physical engagement.

Standing up, you approach the heavy bag - a hulking cylinder of thick leather, mottled and scuffed from years of fists meeting its surface. It hangs from chains, swaying slightly with the movement of the old ceiling fans above. The sound is a low, steady creak, like the steady heartbeat of the space. The bag smells of resin and sweat, the scent of countless sessions where anger and frustration were met and metered out in blows.

You place your feet firmly on the ground, feeling the cold rubber mat press against the soles, your toes spreading wide like roots searching for grip. The stance you settle into is a warrior’s posture: left foot forward (if you’re right-handed), weight balanced but ready to shift. Your knees are slightly bent, the quadriceps engaged but not locked. You feel the subtle tension in your calves, the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles coiled beneath the skin, ready to push, jump, pivot. The hips are squared but loose, the pelvis tilted just so to keep your center of gravity low and stable.

Your fists come up, wrapped bones now encased in gloves, the knuckles aligned to absorb impact. The wrist feels supported, the wrap and glove working together like a scaffolding, protecting the delicate joint from the force you’re about to unleash. You flex your fingers inside the glove, feeling the metacarpals and phalanges align, the weight of the glove pressing into the soft tissue of your palm. The subtle shift of the extensor muscles along the back of your forearm signals readiness.

The first punch is never rushed. There’s a moment - a stillness - where the body locks into place, the breath draws inward, the diaphragm contracts, and the shoulder, led by the deltoid and rotator cuff muscles, rotates in a fluid motion. Your eyes narrow slightly, focusing on the leather target. The knuckles of your first strike meet the bag with a solid thud, the vibration traveling up the radius and ulna, through the wrist, and settling into the forearm muscles. The bag swings, a pendulum marking the rhythm of your release.

In this ritual, you are not just fighting an external opponent. You are meeting the weight of your own history - the tightness in the masseter, the clenched jaw of old stories told in whispers and silences. The wraps are more than cotton; they are armor for the bones that have carried your fury, the foundation beneath the flesh where your power begins. The stance is more than balance; it is a reclaiming of ground lost to shame and silence. The bag is more than leather and stuffing; it is a receptacle for all the fury you were taught to bury.

And as you breathe out, muscles releasing, sweat beginning to bead along your hairline and the nape of your neck where the levator scapulae meets the skull, you remember: the body knows. The jaw remembers. This is not pathology, this is biology. The fight is already inside you, and now, finally, it has a place to land.

## The Impact and the Echo

You feel it before your fist even connects - the coil of tension tight as a drawn bowstring in your forearm, the subtle bracing of your trapezius and levator scapulae, muscles waking into a fierce readiness. The air in the gym is thick with the musk of worn leather and the faint, bitter tang of sweat, the evening light slanting low through the windows, dust motes floating like suspended time. Your knuckles meet the heavy bag with a solid thud, a sound that reverberates not just in the room but deep inside you, in the marrow of your bones and the thick fascia that wraps your ribs.

This is not mere exercise. This is the body speaking - loud, clear, unapologetic. The strike lands like a message sent straight through the noise of doubt and old shame, carried by the sinewy fibers of your arm and the grit in your breath. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, pulses as it descends sharply, driving air out in a sharp exhale that releases tension as sure as the fist releases pent-up fury. The breath doesn’t just move - you can feel it rippling through the psoas, that deep core muscle that holds your center together, wringing out the tight knots of withheld anger and years of “don’t make a scene.”

Karla McLaren writes of anger as a boundary-restoring messenger, and here in the moment of impact, you understand it viscerally. Your body is saying, “This far, and no farther.” With each strike, you redraw the lines of your own territory. The jaw clenches briefly - the masseter muscle contracting like a gate slammed shut - but then, as the strike echoes, it relaxes, the hold loosening just enough to let the truth slip out. Sometimes, tears come - not because you are weak, but because the body remembers the long years of suppression, the threads of rage tangled with grief and fear. Tears are the saltwater that cleanses the wound, not a sign of defeat.

Your breath catches, stutters, then steadies into a rhythm that matches the pulse of your heartbeat hammering in your ears. The diaphragm’s dance becomes a drumbeat of liberation, a rhythm that fills the empty spaces left by silence and self-denial. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your nervous system wakes up, shakes off the frozen hold of old trauma, the fascia loosening like dried glue. You feel the trapezius ease, the knots unravel in your neck and shoulders. The heavy bag swings back, a pendulum marking the passage of this moment, this reclaiming.

The impact sends ripples through your entire being. The subtle vibration travels down your arm, through the connective tissue, into the nerves that hum beneath your skin. Your skin smells of earth and sweat, the faint bitterness of dried blood orange from the herbal salve you rubbed into your knuckles before class - plant medicine holding the muscles, the tendons, and the very nerves that tremble beneath your skin. You honor the body’s knowing, because it is the body that carries the story, not just the mind.

In this sacred space of recovery, herbs are not magic potions but serious medicine, each molecule a thread in the tapestry of healing. You don’t rush this process. You hold space for it in the deep hours of night when the world is quiet and the body speaks loudest. And in that holding, you reclaim what was always yours - the right to rage, to rest, and to rise again whole.  

If you want to explore some of these herbs, here are a few trusted sources: [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20), [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20), [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20). Use them with respect, listen to your body, and remember - this is not pathology, this is biology.

## Frequently Asked Questions

You come to boxing carrying a whole lifetime of whispers and warnings about your fury. Maybe your mother’s voice echoes in your jaw, tight in the masseter and along the edges of your temporal bones, saying “Don’t be angry, it’s ugly.” Maybe your shoulders - your trapezius, levator scapulae - hunch instinctively at the hint of confrontation, bracing like a drawn bowstring, ready to snap. So it’s no surprise that when you first hear about boxing, your mind scrambles with questions rooted in old fears: Will this make me more violent? Will I hurt myself? Will the fury I was taught to hide become a monster I can’t control? The answers live in the body’s wisdom, in the slow unwinding of fascia and muscle, in the breath that moves like a river under your ribs. This is not pathology, this is biology.

**Q: Won’t boxing make me more violent or angry?**

**A:** This is one of the most common fears, and it’s understandable. You’ve been taught that fury is dangerous, ugly, something to suppress or hide. But the body knows better. When you step into the ring, you’re not unleashing some wild beast; you’re offering your nervous system a chance to engage with that energy in a contained, rhythmic, and profoundly somatic way. The masseter and temporalis muscles, often clenched tight with suppressed rage, get to work in a controlled, deliberate manner. The diaphragm breathes deeper and steadier, releasing tension that’s been stuck in the chest and belly for years. Your brain shifts from the freeze or flash of panic to the focused alertness of the present moment. Teachers like Diane Dreher and Stanley Keleman remind us: the jaw remembers, but it also learns new patterns. Boxing teaches the body how to move fury through action rather than letting it fester in the neck or the gut. This is not about becoming violent; it’s about reclaiming your power and expressing anger as a healthy, necessary part of your being.

**Q: I’m worried about hurting my hands or wrists. Isn’t boxing hard on them?**

**A:** Your hands are marvels of anatomy, supported by intricate networks of tendons, ligaments, and muscles. Yes, improper technique or lack of preparation can lead to injury - but that’s true of any physical practice. When you work with a knowledgeable coach who emphasizes proper wrapping of the hands, the alignment of the wrist, and the engagement of the forearm muscles, you’re actually training your body to move safely. The extensor and flexor muscles of the forearm learn to absorb impact, and the connective tissue around the carpals strengthens over time. The skin smells like sweat and leather, a gritty reminder of the work your body is doing to re-pattern itself. Remember: this is not reckless pounding; it’s a dialogue between your intention and your tissues. People who come from years of tension in the shoulders and neck often find that boxing, done well, releases old fascial restrictions and teaches new motor patterns that protect the hands and wrists rather than harm them.

**Q: How do I know boxing is somatically safe for me?**

**A:** Somatic safety is about listening to the body’s signals and respecting its boundaries. When you box, you’re engaging muscles like the psoas and trapezius in ways that open and strengthen rather than constrict and injure. The practice is about rhythm, breath, and precision - not brute force. When you learn to tune into the subtle shifts in your fascia and muscles, you begin to notice the difference between productive effort and harmful strain. Good boxing teachers - those who integrate somatic principles - encourage you to notice the quality of your breath, the way your jaw and neck soften between punches, how your feet root into the earth when you shift your weight. The body’s wisdom guides you away from injury. This is not about pushing through pain or ignoring warning signs; it’s about creating a practice that honors your biology and fosters resilience.
